title: China’s Open AI Models Are Advancing Its Global Soft Power | NOEMA
gist: Andrew Ng argues China's strategy of releasing open-weight AI models (DeepSeek, GLM 5.2) is winning global adoption, especially in Africa and other nations wary of relying on U.S. closed models — turning open-weighting into a soft-power and supply-chain lever just as the U.S. briefly yanked foreign access to Anthropic's Fable 5, which accelerated other nations' urgency to secure sovereign AI supply.

insights.claim: China's AI companies have pursued a strategy of releasing open-weight models (full published weights, freely downloadable) while the leading U.S. labs — OpenAI, Anthropic, Google — keep their frontier models closed-weight and prompt-only.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: Open-weighting is a distribution and adoption strategy, not just a technical choice — investors should track weight-openness as a leading indicator of which AI ecosystem captures downstream global market share.
insights.quote: One thing that China has done really well is that a lot of its companies are releasing open-weight models, meaning they’re published on the internet free for anyone to use, including American companies.
insights.locator: “One thing that China has done really well…” paragraph

insights.claim: Open-weighting accelerates knowledge diffusion within a company's own ecosystem, which Andrew Ng credits as a key reason China has closed the capability gap with the U.S. in AI over the last few years.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: Faster internal diffusion from openness is a compounding advantage — watch whether Chinese labs' pace of capability gains continues to outstrip closed-model labs over time.
insights.quote: For Ng, this is a “brilliant move” by Chinese companies because open-weight models rapidly increase the rates of knowledge diffusion within their companies and ecosystem.
insights.locator: paragraph after first Ng quote block

insights.claim: As of mid-2026, U.S. closed-weight models remain ahead of Chinese models on raw capability, but China leads the world in open-weight models, and nations seeking an open alternative are adopting Chinese models instead of American ones.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: The capability lead of U.S. labs is not translating into global adoption share where openness matters — a bifurcated market investors should model separately from raw benchmark leadership.
insights.quote: Right now, the U.S.’s closed-weight models are ahead of the Chinese models, but China leads the world in open-weight models. What this means is that a lot of nations that want an open alternative are adopting Chinese models.
insights.locator: “over the last few years China has raced ahead…” paragraph

insights.claim: In Africa, Chinese models like DeepSeek are already adopted far more widely than American AI models, which Andrew Ng says have fallen behind in that region.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: Africa is a live case study of where open-weight distribution is already winning market share from U.S. incumbents — a bellwether for other price- and access-sensitive regions.
insights.quote: In nations across Africa, noted Ng, “the Chinese DeepSeek and a handful of other Chinese models are adopted very widely, far more than American models, which have really fallen behind.”

themes.theme: AI as soft power and supply-chain leverage

insights.claim: Whichever nation's AI model a user queries will shape the values embedded in the answer (e.g., how a model responds to a question about Tiananmen Square), making model distribution a direct soft-power channel analogous to Hollywood storytelling.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: Model market share abroad is a proxy for cultural and political influence, not just commercial revenue — relevant to how governments and firms weigh AI export policy.
insights.quote: When someone asks a question of AI, such as what happened in Tiananmen Square in 1989, whose model they are using will cause the model to give an answer that reflects that nation’s values. So getting your stuff out there is a tremendous source of soft power influence.
insights.locator: “First… storytelling we’ve seen in Hollywood…” paragraph

insights.claim: Because AI is becoming a core layer of the software supply chain, growing global reliance on Chinese-dominant AI models would weaken America's ability to steer how the technology develops.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: Frame AI adoption geography as a supply-chain dependency risk, similar to semiconductors or telecom equipment, with long-run implications for tech-policy leverage.
insights.quote: AI is a key part of the supply chain of how we build a lot of software products. If more and more companies are building on a Chinese-dominant supply chain, that has implications for America.
insights.locator: “Second, ‘AI is a key part of the supply chain…’” paragraph

insights.claim: The White House's temporary shutdown of foreign access to Anthropic's Fable 5 model (later reversed after review) signaled to the world that the U.S. can unilaterally revoke access to frontier AI, which accelerated other nations' urgency to secure sovereign AI supply via open-weight alternatives.
insights.speaker: Andrew Ng
insights.so_what: U.S. export-control actions on frontier models carry a soft-power cost by pushing allies and neutral states toward Chinese open-weight models as an insurance policy — a second-order effect worth weighing against the security rationale for such controls.
insights.quote: This has accelerated, in many capitals around the world, the urgency with which they feel like they need to secure their own supply of AI … causing many nations to look at open-weight models because once they have all the numbers no one can take it away from them.

insights.claim: David Sacks, co-chair of Trump's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology, publicly acknowledged that Z.ai's GLM 5.2, a Chinese open-weight model, is now competitive with the best available OpenAI and Anthropic models.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: A frontier-level Chinese open-weight model achieving parity, confirmed by a senior U.S. policy official, is a concrete signal that the capability gap referenced elsewhere in the piece is narrowing quickly.
insights.quote: We now have a Chinese open-weight model that is as good as the currently available models from OpenAI and Anthropic.
insights.locator: “Reinforcing Ng’s point, last week David Sacks…” paragraph

themes.theme: Cultural bias and censorship in LLMs

insights.claim: Every large language model, not just Chinese ones, carries the imprint of its cultural-political origin: Chinese models censor Party criticism, Western models exercise culturally driven censorship on race and gender speech, and models built in the Islamic world censor blasphemy against the Prophet Muhammad.
insights.speaker: Kai-Fu Lee
insights.so_what: Investors and policymakers should treat 'model bias' as a universal, region-specific feature of LLMs rather than a China-specific flaw — relevant to how AI governance debates get framed.
insights.quote: Different cultural zones with different values will censor different things. While the Chinese state might censor any criticism of the Party, in the West there is a kind of culturally driven censorship over sensitive speech on race and gender. In the Islamic world, there will be censorship over blasphemy against the Prophet Muhammad.
insights.locator: Kai-Fu Lee quote on cultural-political censorship

insights.claim: It is precisely the local adaptability of China's open-source models — alongside their lower cost — that constitutes their competitive advantage in diffusion, rather than raw capability alone.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: Cost and localizability, not benchmark scores, may be the decisive variables in global AI model market share — a different competitive axis than the one U.S. labs are optimizing for.
insights.quote: Yet it is precisely the adaptability of China’s open-source models to local circumstances that, along with cost, is its competitive advantage.

themes.theme: Proliferation risk and AI arms-control analogy

insights.claim: Former Google CEO Eric Schmidt is more concerned about the proliferation risk of open-source AI models being misused by repressive states, terrorist networks and fraudsters than about the U.S.-China capability race itself, and believes China shares this concern about its own government being targeted.
insights.speaker: Eric Schmidt
insights.so_what: A prominent tech leader flags open-model proliferation, not the closed-vs-open competitiveness gap, as the bigger downside risk — worth tracking as a driver of future AI safety regulation.
insights.quote: I’m much more concerned about the proliferation of open source. And I’m sure the Chinese share the same concern about how it can be misused against their government as well as ours.

insights.claim: Eric Schmidt proposes safety guardrails on open-source models be hardened via RLHF so they cannot be 'backed out' by bad actors, framing this as a technical prerequisite for safely releasing open-weight AI.
insights.speaker: Eric Schmidt
insights.so_what: RLHF-hardened guardrails are proposed as the technical mitigation for open-weight proliferation risk — a benchmark against which future open-weight model releases could be judged.
insights.quote: We need to make sure that open-source models are made safe with guardrails in the first place through what we call ‘reinforcement learning from human feedback’ (RLHF) that is fine-tuned so those guardrails cannot be ‘backed out’ by evil people.
insights.locator: “We need to make sure that open-source models…” paragraph

insights.claim: Schmidt proposes a Cold War-style 'no-surprise rule' between the U.S. and China: each side would notify the other before training a genuinely new frontier-level AI model, as a first step toward mutual transparency on AI capabilities.
insights.speaker: Eric Schmidt
insights.so_what: A concrete, if speculative, arms-control-style proposal for U.S.-China AI de-escalation — worth monitoring for any diplomatic traction given how unlikely broader AI curbs agreements are.
insights.quote: One thing I think both sides should agree on is a simple requirement that, if you’re going to do training for something that’s completely new on the AI frontier, you have to tell the other side that you’re doing it. In other words, a no-surprise rule.
insights.locator: “One thing I think both sides should agree on…” paragraph

insights.claim: The author frames the current U.S.-China AI race as running 'neck and neck' and argues this moment of converging interest in guardrails is the most propitious time for the two sides to act on establishing shared rules.
insights.speaker: NATHAN GARDELS
insights.so_what: A close-race framing implies neither side has enough of a lead to unilaterally dictate terms, which is the author's rationale for why guardrail negotiations could actually happen now rather than later.
insights.quote: The global AI race is running neck and neck. It is at this moment — when each side can see the convergent interest in adopting common guardrails — that is the most propitious time to act.
